note: This CME is visible as a northwest partial halo in STEREO A COR2 imagery, and a northeast halo in SOHO LASCO C2/C3 and GOES CCOR-1 imagery. Although no evidence of an eruption was visible on the Earth-facing disk, the Solar Orbiter STIX instrument, which was located at longitude -155 at the time of this event, observed the equivalent of a ~C9 flare peaking at 2026-05-30T08:01Z. Additionally, STEREO Ahead observed an increase in the 13-100 MeV protons starting around 2026-05-30T09:00Z, with a peak value of 0.02 pfu/MeV observed at 2026-05-30T10:15Z. It is possible the source is related to former Active Region 14436, located at N18E150 at the time of this event, based on prior activity from this region when it was on the Earth-facing disk. Additionally, the triangulated CME measurement places the CME in the vicinity of former Active Region 14436.

note: Bright loop CME first seen to the East by SOHO LASCO C2 beginning at 2026-06-02T12:12Z, as well as by SOHO LASCO C3, STEREO A COR2 and GOES CCOR-1 in later frames. This CME has a faint, diffuse, preceding feature, but not a shock, seen ahead of the main bulk, likely due to the nature of the source eruption. The source of this event is a very gradual filament eruption seen centered at approx. S10E75 as seen in GOES SUVI, STEREO A EUVI and SDO AIA 304. This filament becomes unstable beginning at approx. 2026-06-02T10:30Z and gradually rises off the limb, but does not begin to disconnect from the disk until approx. 2026-06-02T12:00Z. This earlier rise likely accounts for the diffuse feature seen in coronagraphs before the main bulk of the filament ejecta. Field line opening, dimming and post eruptive arcades are also visible in association with this eruption in SDO AIA 171, 193, 211 and GOES SUVI 284.

The second and less likely candidate is some additional minor ejecta from AR 14455 following CME: 2026-06-02T17:23Z, but measurement indicates that this second source candidate is not a good fit.

The half-width of the CME is based on later imagery in STEREO A COR2, as in coronagraphs there is/are later, wider, lobe(s) of this CME that seem to be integral part of it.

note: Far-sided halo CME to the SE in SOHO LASCO C2/C3 and GOES CCOR-1, and wide front observed to the W in STEREO A COR2. The source is more directly observed in STEREO A EUVI 195/304 on or beyond STEREO's limb (~W149.9) as a filament eruption and field line movement starting around 2026-06-05T08:15Z. It is also observed in GOES SUVI 195/284, and less prominent in SDO AIA 171/193/211 with its closer field-of-view, as wide opening field lines and EUV wave over the SE limb starting around 2026-06-05T09:00Z. Latest Solar Orbiter EUI 304 imagery at 2026-06-0500:01Z shows numerous filaments resting around the W180-E150 and N15-S30 range surrounding former Active Regions 14439 and 14442, for potential candidates. Likely associated with an estimated M3 [M2,M6] flare observed by Solar Orbiter STIX peaking at 2026-06-05T08:58Z.

The eruption is best seen in SDO AIA 193 as moving field lines and a large area of dimming directed toward the south. The eruption is also accompanied by C2.3 class flare from AR 14461 that peaked at 2026-06-09T19:18Z best seen in SDO AIA 131. The full extent of the eruption is better visualized in GOES SUVI 284 imagery, where a faint loop seen stretching from northeast to southeast begins erupting outward off the eastern limb.

The source of this event is a small eruption from Active Region 14475 (S09E32) beginning at approx. 2026-06-24T10:00Z as seen in SDO AIA 131 and 193.

note: CME observed to the SW in STEREO A COR2, not yet visible in SOHO LASCO C2/C3 due to a downlink gap and GOES CCOR-1 due to a data gap. Observed as filament ejecta over the SW limb in SDO AIA 304 starting at 2026-06-25T13:25Z and also in GOES SUVI 304. Observed on-disk brightening and field line movement in STEREO A EUVI 195/304 starting at 2026-06-25T13:25Z near former AR 14469, putting the approximate center filament location ~S15W115. Also observed as field line movement and filament ejecta in SDO AIA 131/171/193.

The first is some minor field line movement near the SE limb in SDO AIA 171 beginning as early as 2026-06-26T17:48Z. The second candidate is near-continuous activity and fluctuation from AR 14478 that is best seen in SDO AIA 193 for hours prior to and following the CME start time.

The most likely source candidate for this CME is a region of southward directed dimming best seen in SDO AIA 195 starting around 2026-06-26T17:34Z centered around S30E05. There is also some field line movement likely associated with this dimming over the S limb best seen in SDO AIA 171 beginning around 2026-06-26T20:06Z and in GOES SUVI 284 beginning around 2026-06-26T19:49Z. A much less likely source candidate for this CME is associated with a C2.4 class flare from AR 14475 (S09W04) that peaked at 2026-06-26T20:21Z. The flare is best seen in SDO AIA 131, but the eruption followed by some post eruptive arcades is best seen in SDO AIA 304 and SDO AIA 195.

note: CME observed to the E only in STEREO A COR2, likely due to being directed towards the SOHO and GOES occulting disks. This could also correspond to a brightening loop in SDO AIA 193 starting around 2026-06-27T15:33Z centered ~N05E26 between AR 14479 and AR 14478.

This CME front carries some uncertainty with it as the leading edge in STEREO A COR2 overlaps with the same triangulation for CME: 2026-06-27T19:12Z, which features a front to the SE in SOHO and GOES. The source for this CME is likely far-sided, with a newly developed active region located around W170-W180 (previously located ~W150-W160 at 2026-06-26T07:09Z in Solar Orbiter PHI). It may be visible in STEREO A EUVI 195 over its west limb starting 2026-06-27T15:05Z, but this is quite early. Solar Orbiter STIX observed an estimated GOES-equivalent X1 [M6, X2] flare from this approximate area of the disk peaking at 2026-06-27T18:20Z.

cmeAnalyses.note: Measurement based on potential source location and the best fit between SOHO LASCO C2/C3 and STEREO A COR2 in SWPC_CAT. The leading edge is not clearly measurable in GOES CCOR-1 imagery.

note: CME visible to the east in GOES CCOR-1 and faintly to the west in STEREO A COR2 imagery. The CME is not seen in real-time in SOHO LASCO C2/C3 due to a downlink data gap. The source is likely farsided as seen as moving field lines in SDO AIA 171 in ISWA and SDO AIA 193 starting around 2026-06-29T06:57Z. An estimated M4.0 class solar flare was observed on the farside from the point of view of the Solar Orbiter/STIX, located around longitude -158, at 2026-06-29T07:29Z.

cmeAnalyses.note: Measurement based on the best fit between GOES CCOR-1 and STEREO A COR2 imagery in SWPC_CAT. The leading edge is faint and unclear in running difference imagery, and this measurement is approximate.
